messages.body:
Notes from “Daily checkin”

These notes have been sent to invited guests in your organization.

Open meeting notes

The content was auto-generated on April 1, 2026, 4:45 PM WAT and may  
contain errors.


Summary
Architectural proposal for continuous improvement of email and scheduling  
style guides was presented with discussion regarding atomic changes and  
approval for implementation.


Continuous Improvement Architecture Review
The proposed architecture for continuous improvement involves a weekly  
crunch job aggregating user data to update email and scheduling style  
guides based on persistent user edits. The 5-step process uses cosine  
similarity to classify changes as persistent, defined as being seen more  
than 3 times, before passing them to an update agent.

Clarifying Atomic Changes Logic
Atomic changes are defined as small adjustments to a user's learned  
preferences, addressing new persistent patterns with minor updates, rather  
than template rewrites. The update agent uses an LLM call to reason over  
the input and desired changes, with a human-in-the-loop step for reviewing  
and queuing proposed updates.

Architectural Approval and Simplification
Clarification was provided that the reject changes tool means no updates  
will be made to the current user configuration, leading to an agreement to  
simplify the interface by combining redundant tool calls. The architecture  
was generally approved, with the decision made to move forward with  
implementation.


Suggested next steps



[Triumphant Babalola] Simplify Interface: Remove the redundant no changes  
needed action from the interface plan.

[Triumphant Babalola] Implement Architecture: Implement the continuous  
improvement architecture plan. Notify Henry Dowling when the Pull Request  
is ready for merging.

[Triumphant Babalola] Request Assistance: Notify Henry Dowling if  
implementation efforts are blocked or require support.

The content was auto-generated on March 31, 2026, 6:27 PM WAT and may

Summary
Authentication error fixes facilitated the system's ability to pull draft  
data, with a deep dive into continuous learning methodologies.


Authentication Error Fixes
An authentication error involving the Google ID was fixed, enabling the  
scheduling feature to work via email. A system preference for scheduling  
and email style is being built, but insufficient data is currently  
available.

Researching Continual Learning Approach
Discussion centered on the complex challenge of distinguishing between  
instance-specific feedback and persistent user preferences in memory  
systems. The decision was made to prioritize continuous learning by  
updating the per-user email and scheduling preference guides.

Technical Implementation Strategy
The agreed-upon technical strategy involves using weighted classifiers on  
diffs to track consistent changes over 30 days and update the user style  
guides. The system must address the memory leak causing the production  
service to crash every 15 minutes.


Suggested next steps



[Triumphant Babalola] Test Scheduling: Send a scheduling-related email to  
verify draft appearance in the inbox after the call.

[Triumphant Babalola] Build Continuous: Build the RL layer, including  
weighted measures and classifiers, to extract insights from email  
differences.

[Triumphant Babalola] Prepare Comparison: Put the output of the continuous  
learning experiment into an Excel sheet to compare reasons for required  
changes.

[Triumphant Babalola] Request Production: Reach out for access to the  
production database for collected draft and sent email data once initial  
plumbing is complete.

[Triumphant Babalola] Create Test: Use AI to mock up synthetic user data  
and exchanges for initial testing of the continuous learning system.

[Samuel Liu] Fix Server: Investigate and fix the server memory leak causing  
frequent crashes.

messages.subject: Welcome to Scheduled!
messages.body:
Hello and welcome to Scheduled!

We're excited you are here! Our goal is to empower you to focus on what matters by automating away the mind-numbing tasks that have become our lives. We image a world where humans don't have to spend another minute doing such mundane tasks (trust us, one of us used to be a management consultant, so we've had our fair share of sleep-less nights aligning boxes and scheduling meetings).

Unlike other scheduling tools like Calendly, Cal.com, or Fxyer, our product philosophy is built around seamlessly fitting into your existing scheduling preferences and workflow. If you head over to your settings, you will find guides based on what we have learned from how you have scheduled in the past. These will be used by our agent to help you schedule future meetings.

Note that we take privacy very seriously and this is the only personal data of yours we store on our server, which can be verified by looking through our open source codebase.

We noticed you tend to keep your schedule lean and purposeful — short daily syncs paired with longer focused sessions when deeper work is needed. We've set things up to match that rhythm so your most important recurring commitments stay protected automatically.

You are all set for now. Expect to see drafts written by our agent pop-up automatically in threads where scheduling is a concern. We handle all of the background work of checking against your calendar, preferences, and sending invites for you. Feel free to edit these drafts or just send as is.

If this resonates, we would love to hop on a quick 15 minute call to get you up to speed and to see how else we can build this to match your needs. Just reply to the email (there should be a draft auto-populated below) and we'll use scheduled to automatically find a time.

Warmly,

Sam
CEO and Co-Founder of Scheduled by Fergana Labs

Notes from “Daily checkin”

Open meeting notes

The content was auto-generated on March 30, 2026, 6:38 PM WAT and may

Summary
Working relationship reset for increased engineering velocity, with a new  
continual learning project for the emailer and discussion of the AI agent  
update strategy.


Velocity and Relationship Reset
Henry Dowling apologized for reduced bandwidth and proposed a working  
relationship reset, requesting higher engineering velocity from Triumphant  
Babalola. Henry Dowling committed to providing more detailed feedback and  
specs to improve efficiency and work on the new project.

Continual Learning Emailer Project
A new project was introduced to build continual learning for the emailer  
scheduling product to update the user's style guide and scheduling  
preferences over time. The system will continually read sent and received  
emails, attempting to update the guides at least once per 24 hours.

Update Strategy and Plumbing
The key metric for determining guide updates is the diff between the  
agent-generated draft and the final email sent by the user, noting  
performance concerns for agents with too much style content. The first  
engineering step is to ensure the system can grab sent emails and match  
them to drafts, which should be done using the thread ID.


Suggested next steps



Triumphant Babalola will set up a cron job to run the style guide updater  
agent in the back end, initially once every 24 hours for all users.

Triumphant Babalola will build a system to continually read the user's sent  
and received emails to update the style guide and scheduling preferences  
guides.

Triumphant Babalola will familiarize with the plumbing to read user inboxes  
and sent emails, and match the diffs between the drafts created and the  
sent emails.

Henry Dowling will share the Google doc about the project with Triumphant  
Babalola.

Triumphant Babalola will try to sign up for scheduled again tomorrow and  
see if the authentication error persists, then hop on a call with Henry  
Dowling to debug if necessary.

The content was auto-generated on March 25, 2026, 5:44 PM WAT and may

Summary
HTML streaming issues for presentation files discussed, with resolution  
focusing on disabling the redundant file preview mechanism and a new  
internal project introduced.


HTML Streaming Glitch Identified
HTML streaming for presentation files successfully allows slide-by-slide  
viewing in the preview tab, but a glitch occurs due to an overlap between  
the new streaming flow and the existing file saving mechanism. Streaming  
capabilities for follow-up edits were added, though consistency issues with  
persistence remain a current focus for remediation.

Resolve Overlapping Preview
Discussions centered on the conflict between immediate file preview and new  
HTML streaming, resulting in the decision to keep the chat UI preview  
mechanism. The path forward is to instruct the agent to recognize the  
active HTML stream and avoid triggering the file preview again, only  
keeping the file displayed in the chat UI.

Internal Project Introduction
The screen blanking bug will be fixed before submitting a Pull Request to  
avoid broken code, with the proposed fix focusing on preventing redundant  
preview triggering. A new internal project involving an email assistant,  
which originated as a recruiting tool, was introduced as a potential  
complement to the existing PowerPoint product.


Suggested next steps



Henry Dowling will give Triumphant Babalola a tour of the email product  
tomorrow.

Triumphant Babalola will fix the overlapping glitch between the file  
showing on the UI itself and the streaming preview being persistent before  
putting up a PR.

Henry Dowling will review the PR once Triumphant Babalola is done with the  
current fix.

Summary
Pull Requests were merged to unblock team research, followed by extensive  
debugging of persistent Versel deployment issues, which culminated in a  
successful resolution.


Pull Requests Merged
Two outstanding Pull Requests were reviewed and deemed good to merge, which  
was completed to unblock the team and allow research activities to resume.

Resolve Versel Deployment Blocker
Versel deployment access failed due to a conflict between the GitHub setup  
on the personal account and the team account. Debugging revealed that  
GitHub's 'keep my email address private' setting was blocking deployment  
via a non-deployable no-reply email.

Deployment Issues Resolved
Connecting the GitHub account directly to Versel's login connections  
resolved the persistent deployment issue. The team confirmed that the  
deployment was unblocked, and focus shifted to planning for HTML streaming  
research.


Suggested next steps

Summary
PR review and bug fix for Bulk Two demonstrated functionality, confirming  
automatic presentation display is now working and ready for PR, with an  
agreement to use the existing SSE stream to handle Bug Three's streaming  
HTML updates.


Bulk Two Bug Fix Demonstrated
The fix for the "bulk two" bug was demonstrated, confirming users can now  
see the file being saved within the UI and can click to preview the file. A  
smoother experience was also confirmed when navigating between updated  
files and returning to previous ones.

Automatic Presentation Display Confirmed
The original intended behavior of presentations showing up automatically  
without a button press is now functional. This automatic display fix  
resolves the bug, and the solution is ready for a Pull Request.

Strategy for Streaming HTML Updates
For Bug Three involving streaming HTML updates, the team agreed to use the  
existing Server-Sent Events (SSE) stream. The plan is to attach new HTML to  
the existing SSE event whenever a "write tool" signals an edit to the HTML.


Suggested next steps



Henry Dowling will review the PR for the bug fix, change the PR slightly to  
match the buttons better to the design system, and send review comments  
over later today.

Triumphant Babalola will raise the PR for the recently discussed fix and  
then get started on implementing SSE for streaming HTML updates.

Plan

Plan mode in Cursor

Design

Summary
Bug fixing review confirmed resolved UI issues with tab switching and  
presentation display, via focus on database query and React Query cache  
optimization.


Bug Fixes and Progress
Bug fixing is advancing well, including resolution of the UI mapping back  
to an old tab and presentations not showing up in the chat UI. Triumphant  
Babalola confirmed the tab snapping issue is fixed and was requested to  
create a Pull Request immediately.

Optimization Challenge Diagnosis
The current challenge involves slow presentation display due to reading  
directly from the database, which led to a broken code reversion during  
optimization attempts. The delay, initially suspected as a database query  
issue, was diagnosed as a combination of generation latency and a possible  
React Query cache invalidation problem.

Critical Bug Resolution Priority
It was decided that fixing the 3 critical bugs (tab snapping, presentation  
visibility, and slow display) must be completed before returning to  
research work, as these issues are currently blocking development. Henry  
Dowling offered to pair program on the React Query issue and will review  
all subsequent fix submissions.

production and put up PRs for the other two fixes once they are completed.

messages.subject: Notes: “Daily checkin” Mar 16, 2026
messages.body:
Notes from “Daily checkin”

These notes have been sent to invited guests in your organization.

Open meeting notes

The content was auto-generated on March 16, 2026, 6:12 PM WAT and may  
contain errors.


Summary
Meeting focused on critical UI and streaming issues following the product's  
port to a web app with agreement on blocking bugs and a new HTML streaming  
strategy.


Streaming and UI Bugs
Testing revealed significant front-end issues, including slow application  
responses, twitching, and output disappearance, indicating poor streaming  
experience. A critical bug caused tabs to switch automatically, which was  
acknowledged as a major UI flaw.

Presentation Saving Bug Identified
The inability to see image additions and presentation changes in the  
preview was confirmed as the primary blocking bug, due to files saving  
locally in the cloud sandbox and not syncing correctly to the user  
interface. The team decided to halt the HTML import project to prioritize  
fixing these critical saving and display errors.

Streaming HTML Edits Proposed
The current file saving infrastructure, which creates a new presentation  
file on every chat turn, was deemed inefficient. The team proposed moving  
to an infrastructure that streams HTML edits to resolve UI issues and  
provide a real-time display of changes.


Suggested next steps



Triumphant Babalola will work on fixing the tab switching issue in the UI,  
considering getting rid of the tab state machine as a possible solution.

Triumphant Babalola will work on enabling the created HTML presentation  
slide to be previewed directly on the UI without changing the current flow,  
likely by fixing a React Query cache issue.

Triumphant Babalola will explore how to stream the HTML from the write tool  
to update the document preview during mid-turn edits.

Henry Dowling will send a Slack message summarizing the technical  
approaches for fixing the identified UI issues.

messages.subject: Notes: “Daily checkin” Mar 12, 2026
messages.body:
Notes from “Daily checkin”

These notes have been sent to invited guests in your organization.

Open meeting notes

The content was auto-generated on March 12, 2026, 7:10 PM WAT and may  
contain errors.


Summary
Initial setup and authentication errors were debugged via environment  
variable investigation and resolved by disabling the redundant AuthZero  
consent screen.


Initial Environment Setup and Errors
Debugging resolved initial setup issues, including safely deleting the  
cloud presentation agent installation instruction and investigating an  
authentication error that showed "A is not fully configured" despite key  
variables being present. The primary problem stemmed from multiple  
environment variable checks in get ozero client, leading to the  
identification of two missing variables.

Authentication Environment and Log Fixes
The team resolved environment file issues by adding the missing Issuer Base  
URL and restarting the Next environment, which made debug logs appear and  
resolved the initial variable configuration failure. After successful login  
attempts, a new error emerged involving a workspace creation failure  
indicating "user not found in database for Ozero user ID".

AuthZero Loop and Business Model
A persistent authorization loop occurred after accepting the AuthZero  
consent screen, which was successfully reproduced, confirming it was a  
general bug, and the major decision made was to disable the redundant  
consent screen entirely. The business model was confirmed as a consumer  
SaaS model priced at $20 monthly after a 7-day free trial, following a  
Product-Led Growth strategy.


Suggested next steps



Henry Dowling will get back to Triumphant Babalola after the meeting on the  
best way to pay for Cursor and cloud access since Triumphant Babalola is a  
contractor through Turing.

Triumphant Babalola will try to install the necessary package or setting on  
VS Code/Cursor to enable clicking on function definitions.

Triumphant Babalola will ask Cursor why the database is out of sync and  
causing the error.

Henry Dowling will fix the bug with the application logic for signing up  
and send Triumphant Babalola a video showing how to log in once the OATH  
issue is fixed.

Triumphant Babalola will commit the edit made on the requirements for the  
cloud presentation agent to the repository.

Triumphant Babalola will continue research on using a VLM as an agent  
evaluator for the presentation project.

Triumphant Babalola will check the codebase to see if Henry Dowling has  
pushed changes and then try running the application locally again to  
proceed with the project.

# Scheduling Preferences Guide
*For use by an AI scheduling agent — based on calendar events and emails from 2026-01-30 to 2026-03-31*

---

## ⚠️ Important Context: Limited Data Window

This account's email and calendar history begins around **mid-March 2026** (the Gmail workspace welcome email arrived March 11, 2026, and the Slack workspace invitation arrived the same day). This means meaningful scheduling data covers only **~3 weeks**, not the full 60-day window. All patterns below are drawn from this shorter period. Confidence levels are noted accordingly. As more data accumulates, this guide should be updated.

---

## 1. Timezone & Location

- **Primary timezone**: **West Africa Time (WAT) — Lagos, Nigeria (UTC+1)**
- The user's calendar and all meeting notes reflect WAT. Calendar invitations explicitly reference "West Africa Time - Lagos."
- The user's primary counterparties appear to be operating in **US Pacific Time (PDT, UTC-7)**, roughly an 8-hour gap.
- **No in-person meetings or location-specific meetings** were observed in this window. All meetings are fully remote.
- **Confidence: Strong** — timezone and remote-only setup are clearly established.

---

## 2. Recurring Commitments

### Daily Work Check-in (Mon–Fri)
- **Frequency**: Every weekday
- **Time**: ~**5:30 PM WAT** (occasionally adjusted to 5:00 PM WAT)
- **Duration**: **15 minutes** (scheduled slot; actual meetings sometimes run 15–45 minutes based on Gemini note timestamps)
- **Format**: Video call via **Google Meet**
- **Participants**: The user + [manager] (organizer) + one [optional teammate]
- **Purpose**: Technical/engineering standup — progress updates, code review, unblocking issues, and project direction
- **Notes**: Auto-generated by Gemini after each session; recordings also stored via Fyxer for some sessions
- **Confidence: Strong** — 14+ instances observed across the window

This is the **only recurring meeting type** observed. There are no other recurring blocks (no weekly team meetings, no 1:1 reviews, etc.) visible in this window, though this may reflect the newness of the account.

---

## 3. Preferred Meeting Times

- **Evening (WAT)**: All observed meetings fall in the **5:00–6:00 PM WAT** range. The standard slot is **5:30 PM WAT**.
- This corresponds to **9:30 AM Pacific Time** — a typical morning slot for the counterparty. The schedule appears to be set to accommodate the manager's morning rather than the user's preference per se.
- **Morning hours (WAT)** appear to be the user's primary deep-work window — no meetings are scheduled before ~5 PM WAT. This is likely a strong implicit preference for **protected mornings**.
- **One ad-hoc meeting** was observed at **9:00 AM UTC (10:00 AM WAT)** — a product demo/tour with [manager], scheduled as a one-off during the morning. This may indicate morning slots are acceptable for special sessions.
- **Confidence: Moderate** — limited variety of meeting types observed; the end-of-day pattern is likely driven by the cross-timezone scheduling constraint rather than purely personal preference.

---

## 4. Meeting Durations by Type

| Meeting Type | Scheduled Duration | Observed Actual Duration |
|---|---|---|
| Daily work check-in (1:1) | 15 min | 15–45 min (often runs over) |
| Ad-hoc demo / product tour | 60 min | 60 min (one instance) |

- The daily check-ins **frequently run over** their 15-minute slot. Gemini notes often timestamp meeting completion 30–45 minutes after start, suggesting the user is comfortable with open-ended conversations that extend as needed.
- **Confidence: Weak** — only two meeting types observed; more data needed for generalisation.

---

## 5. Meeting Format & Tools

- **Video conferencing**: **Google Meet exclusively** — all observed meetings use a persistent team Google Meet link. No Zoom, Teams, or other platforms were observed.
- **Meeting notes**: Gemini auto-notes are active for all Google Meet calls. Some sessions also use Fyxer call recordings.
- **No in-person meetings** observed in the window.
- **Communication between meetings**: Slack is the primary async channel for the team (the user joined the team Slack workspace on March 11, 2026).
- **Confidence: Strong for Google Meet** — consistent across all sessions. Weak for "no other platforms" due to limited data.

---

## 6. Scheduling Initiation & Patterns

- **The user is primarily a meeting recipient**, not an organizer. All calendar invitations in the window were sent by [manager]. The user has not been observed to initiate meeting invites.
- **No sent scheduling emails** were found in the analysis window, consistent with the user being a more junior/contractor party who receives rather than sets meetings.
- **Lead time**: Invitations are typically sent **same-day or 1 day in advance**, reflecting an informal, fast-moving small-team dynamic rather than a formal booking-ahead culture.
- **Confidence: Moderate** — pattern is consistent but the window is short and the team is very small (2–3 people).

---

## 7. Rescheduling & Conflict Handling

One rescheduling event was directly observed:

> On **March 18, 2026**, the user joined the daily check-in but was caught in traffic while driving home. The meeting was briefly attempted (audio/device troubleshooting occurred), then formally **postponed** to later that evening. The user messaged [manager] when ready, and the meeting was held at approximately **8:30–9:30 PM WAT** — roughly 3 hours after the normal time.

**Key inferences:**
- The user **prefers to reschedule rather than skip** important recurring meetings. Even with a significant disruption (commute + traffic), the meeting was held the same day.
- The daily check-in appears to be treated as a **firm, non-skippable commitment** — it was rescheduled to an unusually late hour (late evening) rather than dropped.
- The user proactively communicated the delay rather than waiting passively.
- **Confidence: Weak** — based on a single observed event. However, the behaviour is consistent with a contractor/employee accountability dynamic.

---

## 8. Protected Blocks & Implicit Boundaries

Based on the absence of meetings in certain windows:

| Time Block (WAT) | Observed Status | Inference |
|---|---|---|
| 12:00 AM – 5:00 PM | Always free | Likely deep work / personal time — **protect this block** |
| 5:00–6:00 PM | Recurring meeting zone | The "meeting window" |
| 6:00–8:00 PM | Generally free | Transition / evening |
| 8:30–10:00 PM | Occasionally used for rescheduled calls | Available in urgent/exceptional cases |

- **Mornings through mid-afternoon (WAT) appear protected** — no meetings have been scheduled here (except one 10 AM one-off). When proposing new meetings, **default to the 5–6 PM WAT window** unless the counterparty is in a timezone that makes this unreasonable.
- **Late evenings (after 9 PM WAT)** should be treated as a last resort and only used when explicitly agreed.
- **Confidence: Moderate** — pattern is consistent but may simply reflect the single counterparty's timezone rather than deliberate preference.

---

## 9. Flexibility Hierarchy (Firmness of Blocks)

From most firm to most negotiable, based on observed behaviour:

1. **Daily work check-in** — Very firm. Rescheduled same-day rather than skipped when disrupted. Treat as near-immovable; if a conflict arises, find a same-day alternative time rather than cancelling.
2. **Ad-hoc project demos / collaborative sessions** — Moderately firm once agreed. Scheduled on relatively short notice by [manager]; likely to be honoured as-is.
3. **Open calendar time** — Flexible; available for new scheduling.

- **Confidence: Weak** — limited variety of events to establish a true hierarchy.

---

## 10. Urgency Signals & Response Patterns

- The user's email activity in this window is primarily **inbound** (Gemini notes, calendar notifications, product newsletters). No outbound scheduling emails were found.
- Communication for scheduling appears to happen via **Slack rather than email** — the team uses Slack for real-time coordination (e.g., "send me a Slack message when you're ready").
- When a meeting needs to shift, the user **communicates the reason and gives a new ETA** (e.g., "stuck in traffic, will message when ready") — a proactive, communicative style.
- **Confidence: Weak** — very few scheduling interactions captured.

---

## 11. What Is NOT Yet Known (Data Gaps)

The following dimensions **cannot be assessed** from current data due to the short history and single meeting type:

- ❓ Preferences for coffee, lunch, or dinner meetings (no social/external meetings observed)
- ❓ Preferred in-person locations or neighbourhoods in Lagos
- ❓ Buffer preferences between back-to-back meetings
- ❓ Preferences for group meetings vs. 1:1s
- ❓ How the user handles external (non-team) scheduling requests
- ❓ How far in advance they prefer meetings to be booked for different types
- ❓ Preferences for meeting on Monday/Friday vs. mid-week
- ❓ Weekend availability

---

## 12. Summary: Scheduling Rules of Thumb

For a future AI agent acting on this user's behalf:

1. **Timezone is WAT (Lagos, UTC+1).** Always convert proposed times to WAT when presenting options.
2. **Default meeting window: 5:00–6:00 PM WAT on weekdays.** This is the established working window for team collaboration.
3. **Protect the morning (before 5 PM WAT)** unless the user explicitly opens it for a session.
4. **Use Google Meet** for all video calls — use the persistent team link or create a new Meet link.
5. **The daily check-in is sacred.** Do not propose anything that conflicts with it without flagging the conflict explicitly. If it must move, find a same-day window.
6. **Async communication via Slack** is preferred for quick scheduling coordination. Email is for formal invitations.
7. **Lead time is short** in this team — 1-day notice for ad-hoc meetings is normal.
8. **If the user is delayed**, the preferred response is to reschedule the same day and communicate proactively, not to cancel.

---

*Guide generated: 2026-03-31 | Analysis window: 2026-01-30 – 2026-03-31 | Effective data start: ~2026-03-11*
*This guide should be updated as more scheduling history accumulates, particularly to fill the data gaps noted in Section 11.*

# Scheduling Email Style Guide
### For: Triumphant Babalola (triumphant@ferganalabs.com)
### Generated: 2026-03-31
### Status: ⚠️ BASELINE — No sent email data available (see Section 0)

---

## Section 0 — Data Availability Notice

> **This guide could not be built from observed sent emails.**

After running 20+ search queries (`from:me`, `from:triumphant@ferganalabs.com`, `in:sent`, and every scheduling keyword variant) and reading every accessible thread, **zero outbound emails from the user were found**. The Gmail integration currently appears to have read access to the inbox only, not the Sent folder.

**What this means for you as the composing agent:**
- All style recommendations below are **inferred defaults**, not empirically confirmed.
- Treat every confidence rating seriously — do not over-weight low-confidence items.
- **Priority action**: As soon as sent-email access is enabled, re-run this analysis and update every section. The biggest unknowns are greeting style, sign-off, and time-proposal format.

---

## Section 1 — What We Know About the User (High Confidence)

From meeting notes (Gemini AI summaries of daily stand-ups), the following is confirmed:

| Fact | Source |
|---|---|
| **Name**: Triumphant Babalola | All meeting notes |
| **Email**: triumphant@ferganalabs.com | Email headers |
| **Company**: Fergana Labs (small startup, ~4 people) | Slack digest |
| **Role**: Software engineer | Meeting notes |
| **Primary contact**: Henry Dowling (manager/co-founder) | All meeting notes |
| **Location/timezone**: WAT (West Africa Time, UTC+1) | Meeting note timestamps |
| **Work rhythm**: Daily 4:30 PM WAT check-ins with Henry | Thread dates |
| **Product context**: Building a scheduling email agent | Meeting notes |

**Implication for scheduling emails**: The user operates in a small, informal startup environment with a single primary collaborator (Henry). Most scheduling interactions are likely low-ceremony, direct, and internal rather than formal external correspondence.

---

## Section 2 — Tone & Formality

**Confidence: LOW** — No sent emails observed. The following is inferred.

### Inferred default: Casual-professional

Given:
- Startup of ~4 people
- Daily informal video check-ins (not formal scheduled meetings)
- Engineering role (typically lower email formality than sales/BD roles)

**Recommendation**: Default to **friendly and direct**. Not overly formal, not too breezy. Think "startup colleague" register — warm but efficient.

**Do:**
- Write like a person, not a corporate template
- Keep things short (2–5 sentences for a scheduling reply)
- Sound helpful and accommodating

**Don't:**
- Use stiff language ("I would like to propose…", "Please advise on your availability")
- Over-apologize or pad with filler
- Use formal salutations like "Dear [Name],"

---

## Section 3 — Greeting Style

**Confidence: LOW** — Not observed. Defaults below.

| Scenario | Recommended Greeting |
|---|---|
| Familiar contact (Henry, team members) | `Hi [First name],` |
| Less familiar external contact | `Hi [First name],` |
| Unknown/cold contact | `Hi [First name],` or `Hello [First name],` |
| Group/team email | `Hi all,` or `Hey team,` |

**Notes:**
- Avoid "Hey" for first-contact external emails until observed otherwise.
- Avoid "Dear" — inconsistent with startup context.
- Do NOT omit the greeting entirely unless the thread is very rapid back-and-forth (chat-like).
- Use **first name only** (not full name) based on the informal team context.

---

## Section 4 — Sign-Off Style

**Confidence: LOW** — Not observed. Defaults below.

| Scenario | Recommended Sign-Off |
|---|---|
| Internal / familiar | `Thanks,` or `Cheers,` |
| External / semi-formal | `Best,` or `Thanks,` |
| Quick reply | `Thanks,` or no sign-off if very short |

**Notes:**
- Avoid "Sincerely," — too formal for this context.
- Henry (the manager) uses `Best,` in his own emails — mirroring this for external contacts is reasonable.
- For one-liner confirmations ("Sounds good, see you then"), a sign-off may be omitted.

---

## Section 5 — How to Propose Meeting Times

**Confidence: LOW** — Not observed. Recommended best-practice defaults below.

### Format: Offer 2–3 specific options inline (not a bullet list, unless 3+ options)

**For 1–2 options (short reply):**
> "I'm free Thursday between 2–4 PM WAT, or Friday morning. Does either work?"

**For 3+ options (slightly longer reply):**
> "A few times that work for me:
> - Tuesday, Apr 2 — 10 AM or 2 PM WAT
> - Thursday, Apr 4 — any time after noon WAT
>
> Happy to adjust if none of those work."

**General rules:**
- Always include timezone (**WAT** or convert for the recipient if they're in a different zone).
- Be specific — give day + time, not just "sometime next week."
- Offer flexibility: end with "or let me know what works for you" / "happy to find another time."
- Don't offer more than 3–4 slots — it creates decision fatigue.

---

## Section 6 — Accepting a Meeting Request

**Confidence: LOW** — Not observed. Defaults below.

Keep it short and warm. Example:

> "Hi [Name], that works for me — see you [day] at [time] WAT. I'll accept the calendar invite."

Or for less formal:
> "[Day/time] works great, talk then!"

**Do:**
- Confirm the specific time back (avoids miscommunication)
- Mention calendar invite if relevant
- Keep it to 1–3 sentences

---

## Section 7 — Declining or Rescheduling

**Confidence: LOW** — Not observed. Defaults below.

Be direct but warm. Offer an alternative immediately — don't just say no.

> "Hi [Name], unfortunately [proposed time] doesn't work for me. Could we do [alternative day/time] instead? Happy to find something that works."

**Do:**
- Always offer an alternative (don't just decline flat)
- Keep the tone apologetic but not over-the-top
- Be specific about what does work

**Don't:**
- Give elaborate explanations for why you're unavailable
- Use passive-aggressive hedging ("I might potentially be available…")

---

## Section 8 — Email Length

**Confidence: LOW** — Not observed.

**Default target: 2–5 sentences** for a standard scheduling reply.

- Simple acceptance: 1–2 sentences
- Proposing times: 2–4 sentences (or a short bullet list)
- Rescheduling/declining: 3–5 sentences
- No scheduling email should exceed ~150 words unless there's a complex multi-party coordination need

---

## Section 9 — Punctuation & Formatting Habits

**Confidence: VERY LOW** — Entirely unobserved.

**Safe defaults:**
- Use standard sentence punctuation (periods, commas)
- Avoid excessive exclamation marks (max 1 per email, only if the context is genuinely enthusiastic)
- No ellipses ("...") unless mirroring the recipient's style
- No ALL CAPS for emphasis
- Light use of bold is fine for calling out a specific time/date, but don't over-format a short email
- Emoji: **avoid by default** unless the recipient uses them first

---

## Section 10 — Common Scheduling Phrases (Suggested Defaults)

The following are reasonable neutral defaults to use until actual phrases are observed:

| Situation | Suggested Phrase |
|---|---|
| Proposing time | "Does [day/time] WAT work for you?" |
| Offering options | "I'm free [option 1] or [option 2] — either work?" |
| Accepting | "That works for me, see you then." |
| Acknowledging flexibility | "Happy to find a time that works for you." |
| Following up | "Just circling back on finding a time to connect." |
| Rescheduling | "Would it be possible to shift to [alternative]?" |

---

## Section 11 — Context-Specific Notes

### Internal emails (Henry, Fergana Labs team)
- Maximum informality within the casual-professional band
- Can skip pleasantries entirely for quick confirmations
- Henry already uses "Best," as a sign-off — matching this is safe

### External emails (new contacts, partners, candidates)
- Step up one notch in formality
- Always include a greeting and sign-off
- Include timezone explicitly (WAT / UTC+1)

### Calendar invites
- If sending a Google Calendar invite alongside an email, mention it: "I'll send over a calendar invite."
- Do not rely solely on the invite without an email — always send a brief human message too

---

## Section 12 — What to Update Once Sent Emails Are Accessible

When sent-mail access is available, re-analyze with priority on:

1. **Actual greeting** — Does the user say "Hi", "Hey", "Hello", or skip it?
2. **Sign-off** — "Thanks", "Best", "Cheers", or none?
3. **Time format** — "2pm", "2 PM", "14:00"? With or without timezone?
4. **Sentence length & rhythm** — Short punchy sentences vs. longer flowing ones?
5. **Exclamation marks** — Does the user use them? How often?
6. **Emoji** — Ever used in professional email?
7. **Contractions** — "I'm / I'll / won't" vs. "I am / I will / will not"?
8. **Any recurring phrases** — Verbal tics or go-to expressions?
9. **How they handle back-and-forth** — Do they quote the previous email? Trim it?
10. **Response speed patterns** — Same-day? Next day? Does this affect tone?

---

*This guide was generated by an AI analyst agent on 2026-03-31. It is a baseline placeholder and should be treated as low-confidence until updated with observed sent email data.*
